misconceptions: Plants are not alive because we cannot see them move
Seeds and eggs are not living things
All living things can move in obvious ways
All things that move are living
Nonliving things can become living on their own
Living things don't need air to survive
Animals don't need water or food to live
Only animals are living things, not plants
Rocks and clouds are living because they change

resources.description: Teacher resource for understanding and addressing common life science misconceptions

applications: Observing schoolyard or neighborhood plants and animals
Growing plants from seeds in the classroom
Nature walks to identify living vs nonliving things
Food chains and what animals eat
How humans interact with living things
Habitats and homes of different animals
best_practices: Use hands-on sorting activities where students categorize objects as living, nonliving, or once-living
Focus on the characteristics all living things share: need for food, water, air; growth; movement; reproduction
Address the misconception that plants are not alive by showing time-lapse videos of plant movement
Incorporate outdoor exploration and nature walks for real-world observation
Use the 5E instructional model (Engage, Explore, Explain, Elaborate, Evaluate)
Start with what students already know and challenge their thinking
Use comparative analysis - have students find patterns in what makes something living
Integrate writing opportunities to explain reasoning about living vs nonliving
Build intervention time for students who need extra support
Use visual aids like anchor charts and picture sorts
Connect to students' lives through pets, gardens, and family experiences
Emphasize inquiry-based learning where students ask questions and seek answers
